scenario: For a long video stream at home,
correctAnswer: Watch in standard definition
wrongAnswers: Watch in 4K
Leave autoplay running afterward
explanation: Standard definition uses less data than 4K, so it usually cuts network and server energy use.

scenario: When you need to download a large file,
correctAnswer: Use a stable Wi-Fi connection
wrongAnswers: Use mobile data if possible
Start the download on two devices at once
explanation: Wi-Fi is typically more energy-efficient than mobile data for large transfers, especially when the connection is stable.

scenario: To keep browser energy use lower during a long work session,
correctAnswer: Close the tabs you are not using
wrongAnswers: Keep every tab open all day just in case
Refresh every open tab often
explanation: Unused tabs still consume memory, processor time, and background network activity.

scenario: When you only need to hear a webinar or music session,
correctAnswer: Use audio-only playback
wrongAnswers: Keep the full video stream on
Open the same stream in two tabs
explanation: Audio-only playback usually transfers far less data than video.

scenario: If you know you will watch the same training clip twice,
correctAnswer: Download it once on Wi-Fi and reuse it
wrongAnswers: Restream it every time
Play the clip on two screens together
explanation: Reusing a file you already downloaded avoids repeated streaming traffic.

scenario: When sharing a presentation with teammates,
correctAnswer: Send a cloud link instead of a large attachment
wrongAnswers: Attach the full file to every email
Send the file in multiple formats to everyone
explanation: Sharing a single link avoids duplicate file transfers and repeated inbox storage.

scenario: On a video call where slides matter more than faces,
correctAnswer: Turn your camera off when it is not needed
wrongAnswers: Keep HD video on the whole time
Open the call on a phone and laptop together
explanation: Sending live video continuously uses more bandwidth and compute than audio alone.

scenario: To avoid passive streaming emissions,
correctAnswer: Turn autoplay off
wrongAnswers: Let the next video start automatically
Keep multiple streams paused in background tabs
explanation: Autoplay can start data-heavy playback you did not actually intend to watch.

scenario: When a phone still works but the battery is weak,
correctAnswer: Repair or replace the battery first
wrongAnswers: Buy a brand-new phone immediately
Keep the old phone and buy a second new one
explanation: Keeping devices in service longer reduces the embodied emissions from manufacturing replacements.

scenario: When reading or writing on a laptop indoors,
correctAnswer: Lower the screen brightness to a comfortable level
wrongAnswers: Keep brightness at maximum
Use an external monitor too when you do not need it
explanation: Display brightness can be a noticeable share of a device's power use.

scenario: While cleaning up cloud storage,
correctAnswer: Delete duplicate files you do not need
wrongAnswers: Keep every duplicate forever
Upload the same folder to multiple personal accounts
explanation: Unnecessary duplicate storage and syncing adds avoidable digital overhead.

scenario: Before uploading a batch of casual photos,
correctAnswer: Compress or resize them when full resolution is unnecessary
wrongAnswers: Upload the largest originals every time
Upload both the originals and duplicates
explanation: Smaller files reduce transfer, storage, and repeated sync costs.

scenario: If a device will sit unused for hours,
correctAnswer: Let it sleep or shut it down
wrongAnswers: Leave it fully on all day
Keep video or music playing in the background
explanation: Idle devices still consume electricity, especially if the screen and apps stay active.

scenario: Before publishing a photo-heavy webpage,
correctAnswer: Compress images and serve responsive sizes
wrongAnswers: Upload every image at original camera size
Use the same oversized image for every screen
explanation: Smaller, responsive images reduce data transfer and page-rendering cost.

scenario: For a site with logos, icons, and style files used on many pages,
correctAnswer: Cache static assets so browsers reuse them
wrongAnswers: Force a full download on every page load
Duplicate the same assets under many filenames
explanation: Caching avoids repeated transfers of identical content.

scenario: When teammates need to review the same document together,
correctAnswer: Use one collaborative cloud document
wrongAnswers: Email new attachments to everyone after each edit
Keep separate copies for every reviewer
explanation: Collaborative editing reduces repeated file copies and sync churn.

scenario: For old files you rarely open but must keep,
correctAnswer: Move them to a colder storage tier
wrongAnswers: Keep everything in high-performance storage forever
Store the same archive in multiple hot locations
explanation: Cold storage is generally more efficient for infrequently accessed data.

scenario: When you only need a clear meeting view and not cinema quality,
correctAnswer: Choose standard video quality instead of the highest HD setting
wrongAnswers: Force the highest available resolution
Share video from two cameras at once
explanation: Higher meeting resolution increases the data processed and transmitted.

scenario: When improving a company website,
correctAnswer: Remove third-party scripts and trackers you do not need
wrongAnswers: Add more external scripts by default
Load several analytics tools that do the same job
explanation: Extra third-party requests add page weight, processing, and network traffic.

scenario: Before a trip with poor connectivity,
correctAnswer: Download maps or reference files once in advance
wrongAnswers: Stream the same map tiles repeatedly on mobile data
Open the same heavy map on several devices
explanation: Preparing data once avoids repeated high-cost downloads later.

scenario: When a phone is about to sync a huge library of raw media,
correctAnswer: Wait for Wi-Fi before uploading
wrongAnswers: Force immediate upload over mobile data
Upload the same library from several devices together
explanation: Large media uploads are usually better handled over efficient, stable connections.

scenario: If two codecs deliver similar viewing quality,
correctAnswer: Choose the more efficient codec
wrongAnswers: Choose the one that needs more bitrate
Stream both versions to compare every time
explanation: More efficient codecs can maintain quality with less data transfer.

scenario: On a content page with images far below the fold,
correctAnswer: Lazy-load the media until users reach it
wrongAnswers: Load all heavy media immediately
Auto-play all media on page open
explanation: Lazy-loading prevents unnecessary downloads for content a user may never view.

scenario: When picking a format for photos on the web,
correctAnswer: Use WebP or AVIF where appropriate
wrongAnswers: Prefer larger legacy formats for every image
Export everything as oversized PNGs
explanation: Modern image formats often deliver the same visual quality with less data.

scenario: When sending regular product updates by email,
correctAnswer: Use a lean template instead of image-heavy layouts
wrongAnswers: Pack every message with large decorative media
Send the same newsletter repeatedly to correct formatting
explanation: Lighter email templates reduce transfer and rendering overhead.

scenario: If users often return to the same pages and assets,
correctAnswer: Use CDN or browser caching effectively
wrongAnswers: Bypass caching on every request
Rebuild and re-send unchanged assets each time
explanation: Caching repeated assets cuts data movement and server work.

scenario: When provisioning cloud compute for a workload,
correctAnswer: Right-size the instance to actual demand
wrongAnswers: Overprovision large instances just in case
Run the job on multiple idle instances without need
explanation: Oversized compute wastes power even when usage stays low.

scenario: For a steady stream of AI inference requests,
correctAnswer: Batch compatible requests where latency allows
wrongAnswers: Process every request separately no matter what
Duplicate the same inference across multiple services
explanation: Batching can improve hardware utilization and reduce wasted compute.

scenario: When a smaller model gives acceptable results for a task,
correctAnswer: Deploy the distilled or quantized model
wrongAnswers: Always use the largest available model
Run both small and large models for every request
explanation: Smaller efficient models often reduce energy use while meeting the need.

scenario: For dashboards that ask the same heavy questions every day,
correctAnswer: Precompute common reports and aggregates
wrongAnswers: Rerun expensive raw queries every page view
Duplicate analytical pipelines for the same output
explanation: Precomputing repeated results lowers repeated compute costs.

scenario: If a database query keeps scanning entire tables,
correctAnswer: Add indexes and tune the query plan
wrongAnswers: Keep the full-table scan as traffic grows
Run the same heavy query more often
explanation: Efficient queries reduce processor, memory, and storage work.

scenario: For a machine-learning system with slow data drift,
correctAnswer: Retrain only when monitoring shows it is needed
wrongAnswers: Retrain from scratch on a fixed schedule regardless
Launch several duplicate retraining jobs
explanation: Unnecessary retraining can consume large amounts of compute for little benefit.

scenario: When backing up production data frequently,
correctAnswer: Use incremental snapshots where possible
wrongAnswers: Create a full backup every time
Store repeated full copies in parallel without deduplication
explanation: Incremental backups avoid copying unchanged data over and over.

scenario: When shipping a modern web application,
correctAnswer: Tree-shake and code-split unused JavaScript and CSS
wrongAnswers: Ship the full bundle to every user
Load every feature module on first page view
explanation: Smaller bundles reduce transfer and client-side processing.

scenario: For static content requested from many regions,
correctAnswer: Serve it through edge caching or a CDN
wrongAnswers: Pull every request from one origin server
Disable edge caching for repeat traffic
explanation: Serving repeated content closer to users usually reduces network distance and origin load.

scenario: When configuring a live stream for a mixed audience,
correctAnswer: Set a sensible default resolution instead of maximum quality for everyone
wrongAnswers: Force the highest quality for all viewers
Stream duplicate feeds at the same time by default
explanation: Not every viewer needs the highest bitrate, and defaults influence total traffic.

scenario: For flexible batch jobs that do not need instant results,
correctAnswer: Shift them to lower-carbon hours when possible
wrongAnswers: Run them immediately during any grid peak
Schedule duplicate jobs across time windows
explanation: Carbon-aware scheduling can use cleaner electricity periods without changing the result.

scenario: When managing large media and backup stores,
correctAnswer: Deduplicate repeated assets and copies
wrongAnswers: Keep identical files in many places without reason
Create extra copies each time someone accesses a file
explanation: Deduplication reduces both storage needs and repeated data movement.

scenario: When latency requirements allow flexible workload placement,
correctAnswer: Run the workload in a region with a cleaner electricity mix
wrongAnswers: Ignore grid carbon intensity completely
Duplicate the workload across high-carbon regions by default
explanation: Where compute runs can matter a lot because electricity carbon intensity varies by region.

scenario: When choosing digital infrastructure partners,
correctAnswer: Prefer providers with credible renewable matching and transparent reporting
wrongAnswers: Rely only on vague marketing claims
Treat generic offsets as equal to transparent operational improvements
explanation: Transparent energy sourcing and reporting are stronger signals than vague sustainability claims.

scenario: When servers still meet performance needs with targeted upgrades,
correctAnswer: Extend their life with selective component replacements
wrongAnswers: Replace the full fleet early without need
Buy parallel spare fleets for the same workload
explanation: Extending hardware life can lower embodied emissions from manufacturing new systems.

scenario: When building an AI assistant with tasks of mixed complexity,
correctAnswer: Route simple requests to a smaller model
wrongAnswers: Send every request to the largest model
Run multiple models for every prompt regardless of need
explanation: Model routing can cut compute use while keeping high-quality results where they matter most.

scenario: When trying to lower service emissions over time,
correctAnswer: Measure grams of CO2e per request and optimize the hotspots
wrongAnswers: Track only uptime and ignore carbon data
Assume all requests have the same environmental cost
explanation: What gets measured can be improved, and request-level hotspots often dominate emissions.

scenario: When tuning autoscaling for a service with quiet overnight periods,
correctAnswer: Reduce always-on minimum capacity where safe
wrongAnswers: Keep large idle minimum capacity all day
Scale up duplicate instances before demand appears
explanation: Idle capacity can quietly waste energy if the floor is set too high.

scenario: For landing pages that aim to load quickly and waste less energy,
correctAnswer: Disable hero-video autoplay by default
wrongAnswers: Auto-play large background video on every visit
Load several hidden videos before user interaction
explanation: Auto-playing media starts heavy transfers even for visitors who may not need them.

scenario: For a mostly static content site with periodic updates,
correctAnswer: Use prerendering or incremental static regeneration
wrongAnswers: Server-render every request even when content rarely changes
Rebuild identical pages for every visitor
explanation: Serving prebuilt content avoids unnecessary per-request compute.

scenario: For educational media watched repeatedly inside one organization,
correctAnswer: Serve it from local cache or nearby edge storage when practical
wrongAnswers: Force every playback to pull the full remote stream
Duplicate the same long stream from multiple remote sources
explanation: Local or edge reuse can reduce repeated long-distance data delivery.

scenario: When defining backup and log retention,
correctAnswer: Keep only what policy and operations require
wrongAnswers: Retain every snapshot and log forever
Copy all retained data into several permanent hot tiers
explanation: Retention discipline reduces long-term storage overhead and needless duplication.

scenario: When designing a multi-region data pipeline,
correctAnswer: Minimize unnecessary duplicate transfers between regions
wrongAnswers: Move the same data repeatedly across regions
Replicate every intermediate dataset everywhere by default
explanation: Cross-region transfer adds network, storage, and processing overhead.

scenario: When solving a new AI task with a strong existing model available,
correctAnswer: Fine-tune or adapt the existing model first
wrongAnswers: Train a large model from scratch immediately
Launch multiple full training runs before testing reuse
explanation: Reusing capable pretrained models can avoid the large compute cost of starting from zero.
